points.text: Reproduce the checkout flow in a staging environment with realistic load to capture latency and retry behavior.
Enable detailed SQL logging and capture the execution plan of the new join to spot scans or missing indexes.
Instrument the checkout code to record start‑end timestamps for DB query, business logic, and payment call.
Verify that each checkout request includes a unique idempotency key and that the payment service respects it.
Temporarily limit client retries to 1 and add exponential backoff to reduce overlapping requests.
Add appropriate indexes on the columns used in the new join; re‑run the EXPLAIN to confirm index usage.
Wrap the payment request in a DB transaction with a UNIQUE constraint on the order ID to prevent double charge.
Deploy monitoring dashboards for checkout latency, retry count, and duplicate‑charge alerts; iterate fixes based on data.
Enable end‑to‑end tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) on checkout request to measure each hop latency.
Log start/end timestamps and duration of the new SQL join; run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to spot missing indexes.
Compare query plan before/after join; add needed indexes or rewrite join to reduce row scans.
Add a request‑level timeout (e.g., 2 s) and circuit breaker around the checkout service to fail fast.
Ensure checkout operation is idempotent; generate a unique order token and reject duplicates.
Reduce client retry count or add exponential backoff; avoid immediate retries that cause double charges.
Run load test with realistic traffic to confirm latency improvements before deploying.
Enable pg_stat_statements and log_min_duration_statement to capture the new join's execution time.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the checkout query to see join type, row estimates, and I/O.
Ensure columns used in the join and WHERE clauses have appropriate btree indexes; add any missing ones.
Check for unintended Cartesian products; rewrite the join with correct ON conditions or subqueries.
Wrap payment updates in a single transaction with SERIALIZABLE or REPEATABLE READ isolation to prevent double charges.
Implement idempotent payment logic (e.g., unique constraint on transaction_id) so retries are safe.
Add exponential backoff on client retries and monitor retry counts to reduce load spikes.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the new join query to identify missing indexes or costly scans.
Monitor lock waits on checkout tables via pg_stat_activity or sys.dm_exec_requests.
Confirm the transaction isolation level; use READ COMMITTED or SNAPSHOT to avoid phantom reads.
Add a UNIQUE constraint on (order_id, attempt_id) to make writes idempotent and stop double charges.
Implement client retries with exponential backoff and include an idempotency key per checkout.
Capture deadlock graphs; if cycles appear, reorder statements or add row‑level lock hints.
Run a load test simulating concurrent checkouts to reproduce latency and pinpoint contention points.
Enable load balancer request/response timing logs to measure latency per checkout request.
Compare LB latency before and after the new SQL join deployment to see if DB change increased response time.
Instrument backend service to log query execution time and identify slow joins or lock contention.
Verify load balancer and server timeout settings; ensure they exceed expected processing time to avoid 502/504 errors.
Add idempotency keys to the checkout API so retries do not create duplicate charges.
Temporarily reduce client retry count or add exponential backoff while investigating the root cause.
Monitor Linux network queues (netstat, ss) for SYN drops or retransmissions that could add latency.
Instrument checkout end‑to‑end latency and capture p95/p99 distributions to quantify the 5–10 s tail.
Add tracing around the new SQL join to measure its exclusive duration versus the rest of the request.
Apply Little’s Law (L = λ·W) to estimate required concurrency given observed average latency W and target throughput λ.
Budget the overall timeout: allocate headroom for DB (e.g., 30 % of total), network, and app processing to keep total <2 s.
Make the charge operation idempotent (e.g., use a unique transaction ID) to prevent double billing on retries.
Reduce client retry aggressiveness or use exponential backoff to lower load spikes during latency spikes.
If the join is the bottleneck, add appropriate indexes or denormalize; test performance before full rollout.
Run a load test at expected RPS, verify that 95 % of requests complete within the SLO (e.g., ≤2 s) after changes.
Enable detailed request and DB timing logs for the checkout endpoint to pinpoint latency sources.
Review the execution plan of the new SQL join and add missing indexes to avoid full scans.
Wrap the entire checkout flow in a single DB transaction with appropriate isolation (e.g., READ COMMITTED).
Introduce a per‑request idempotency key and reject duplicate keys at the API layer.
Create a unique constraint on the order/payment identifier to block duplicate inserts.
Implement exponential back‑off on client retries and return explicit in‑progress error codes.
Use row‑level locking (SELECT … FOR UPDATE) on the payment row before charging.
Set up alerts for checkout latency >2 s and for any duplicate charge events.
